did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid deep learning model combining Xception and EfficientNet CNNs for multi-class skin cancer detection on the HAM10000 dataset. It also employed U-net for lesion segmentation and Enhanced Grad-CAM for visualization.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a hybrid CNN model (Xception + EfficientNet) with U-net segmentation and Enhanced Grad-CAM visualization for multi-class skin cancer detection on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 91.7% accuracy) and includes sections on methodology, results, and discussion, characterizing it as a research study rather than a review.

title: Explainable Deep Learning Multi-Class Skin Cancer Detection using Hybrid CNN and Enhanced Grad-CAM Visualization
journal: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Information and Control Systems (ICOIICS-2025)
brief_summary: This study addresses multi-class skin cancer detection using a hybrid deep learning model that integrates Xception and EfficientNet CNNs on the HAM10000 dataset. To enhance interpretability and feature representation, the framework incorporates U-net for lesion segmentation and Enhanced Grad-CAM for visualization. The model was trained using weighted class balance and evaluated with accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. The proposed hybrid CNN with segmentation achieved an overall accuracy of 91.7%, outperforming individual Xception and EfficientNet models. The Enhanced Grad-CAM visualization provided sharp, localized heatmaps to highlight informative lesion regions, thereby improving model transparency and clinical trust.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin lesions using a hybrid CNN (Xception + EfficientNet) on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories with an overall accuracy of 91.7% (with segmentation) and 89.3% (without segmentation). Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
U-net model used for lesion segmentation to extract the region of interest (ROI) and exclude background noise, which improved classification accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images obtained from the Kaggle platform, consisting of images labeled by expert dermatologists and confirmed by histopathology.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid CNN model (Xception + EfficientNet) with weighted class balance.
use.validation: Used for validation (15% of dataset) to monitor performance and avoid overfitting.
use.testing: Used for testing (15% of dataset) to evaluate final model performance using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
use.other: Used for data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming, illumination adjustment) and resizing to 224x224 pixels.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the dataset into training (70%), validating (15%), and testing (15%) sets while maintaining class proportions.
To handle imbalanced data by ensuring minority classes contributed equally to the loss computation during training.
To visually analyze the distribution of true and predicted classes and evaluate classification performance.
To evaluate the performance of the trained model and describe its classification capabilities.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The Hybrid CNN with segmentation achieved an overall accuracy of 91.7%, outperforming the model without segmentation (89.3%) and individual models (Xception: 88.2%, EfficientNet: 89.1%). The model was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, and parameter count. Enhanced Grad-CAM visualization provided sharp, localized heatmaps.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning and fine-tuning of pretrained Xception and EfficientNet models

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Xception extracts fine-grained spatial details through depth-wise separable convolutions; EfficientNet provides scalable and efficient feature learning across multiple resolutions
architecture_details.fusion_method: Feature maps from intermediate layers of both networks are concatenated to form multi-scale representation
architecture_details.segmentation_module: U-net (encoder-decoder structure) used for lesion segmentation to extract region of interest
architecture_details.classifier_head: Dense layers with ReLU activation and Dropout layers for regularization

training_details.regularization: Dropout, early stopping, and weighted class balance
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, flipping, zooming, and illumination adjustment

classification_or_output_head.visualization: Enhanced Grad-CAM (Grad-CAM++) integrating gradient information from multiple convolution layers

preprocessing_techniques: Hair noise removal (filtering process to eliminate hair noise)
Data augmentation including rotation, flipping, zooming, and illumination adjustment
Lesion segmentation using U-net to extract region of interest (ROI) and exclude background noise

conclusions: The proposed hybrid CNN model (combining Xception and EfficientNet) with U-net segmentation achieved an overall accuracy of 91.7% and an F1-score of 0.87 on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming individual models (Xception: 88.2%, EfficientNet: 89.1%) and the hybrid model without segmentation (89.3%).

Incorporating U-net for lesion segmentation significantly improved classification accuracy by isolating the region of interest and excluding background noise, confirming that focusing on the lesion area enhances model performance.

The Enhanced Grad-CAM (Grad-CAM++) visualization technique provided sharp, localized heatmaps that accurately highlighted the most influential lesion regions, offering superior interpretability compared to conventional Grad-CAM which produces diffuse activation areas.

The integration of explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically Enhanced Grad-CAM, enhances model transparency and clinical trust by allowing dermatologists to verify the model's decision-making process.
The model demonstrated robust generalization and avoided overfitting, as evidenced by increasing training and validating accuracy values across epochs.
Future work will focus on deploying the model for real-time and mobile integration and further enhancing explainability through multi-layer Grad-CAM fusion or SHAP-based reasoning.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance and demographic bias (predominantly fair-skinned populations), limiting the generalizability of the 91.7% accuracy to diverse global populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal test split (15%) of the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific distribution.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) to validate the performance improvements of the hybrid model over baselines.
There is a discrepancy in reported accuracy between the abstract (89%) and the results section (91.7% with segmentation), which may confuse readers regarding the model's true performance.
The dataset was sourced from Kaggle rather than directly from the original authors, which may introduce inconsistencies in data provenance or preprocessing compared to the original HAM10000 release.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope, lacking clinical deployment trials or prospective validation, which are necessary to establish clinical relevance and safety.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while stratified sampling was used, the specific preprocessing steps (e.g., hair removal, augmentation) were applied before splitting, which is standard but requires careful implementation to avoid information leakage.
